1: The Moab oracle (Jeremiah 48) is the longest OAN in Jeremiah and one of the most extensively developed in all prophetic literature. It draws heavily from Isaiah 15-16 and Numbers 21. Lĕmôʾāḇ kōh ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hôy ʿal-Nĕḇô kî šuddādâ (hôy ʿal-Nĕḇô kî šuddādâ): the oracle opens with hôy (woe, funeral cry) over Nebo, a Moabite city on or near Mount Nebo (from which Moses viewed Canaan, Deuteronomy 34:1). Qiryātāyim hōḇîšâ nileḵĕdâ hammiśgāḇ hōḇîšâ ûḥāttâ (hōḇîšâ wĕḥāttâ): Kiriathaim (twin cities, a Reubenite then Moabite town, Numbers 32:37; Ezekiel 25:9) and the stronghold are shamed and shattered — three hōḇîšâ words in this verse and the next create a drumbeat of shame.
2: ʾên ʿôd tehillat Môʾāḇ bĕḤešbôn ḥāšĕḇû ʿālêhā rāʿâ lĕkû wĕnaḵrîtennāh miggôy (ḥāšĕḇû ʿālêhā rāʿâ lĕkû wĕnaḵrîtennāh miggôy): "in Heshbon they have plotted its ruin: Come, let us cut it off as a nation." The wordplay on Ḥešbôn/ḥāšĕḇû (Heshbon/they have devised) — the city's name echoes the verb for scheming — is a classic prophetic omen-name technique (cf. Jeremiah 1:12: šāqēd/šōqēd). Gam-madmēn tiddōmmî ʾaḥărêḵ tēlēḵ ḥāreḇ (Gam-madmēn tiddōmmî): Madmen will also be silenced — the wordplay continues: Madmēn/tiddōmmî (Madmen/will be silenced; dāmam).
3: Qôl ṣĕʿāqâ mîḤōrônāyim šōd ûšeḇer gādôl (Qôl ṣĕʿāqâ mîḤōrônāyim šōd ûšeḇer gādôl): Horonaim ("two caverns," a Moabite town, Isaiah 15:5) — the cry of ruin (šōd ûšeḇer gādôl, devastation and great breaking) heard from there signals the cascade of destruction moving through the Moabite city network. The OAN moves town by town through Moab's geography — a literary technique that creates the effect of a reportage from a moving front line.
4: Nišbĕrâ Môʾāḇ hišmîʿû zĕʿāqâ ṣĕʿirêhā (Nišbĕrâ Môʾāḇ hišmîʿû zĕʿāqâ ṣĕʿirêhā): "Moab is shattered; her young ones raise a cry." Nišbĕrâ (Niphal perfect of šāḇar, to shatter) signals total structural collapse; ṣĕʿirêhā (her little ones) are either children literally weeping or the small towns of Moab crying out. The LXX reads "even to Zoar" which connects to v34, but the MT reading of "young ones/little ones" produces a poignant image of total vulnerability — even children bear the war's destruction.
5: Kî maʿălēh-halûḥît bĕḇekî yaʿăleh-bô kî bĕmôrad ḥōrônāyim ṣārê-yĕlalelû šĕḇer (Kî maʿălēh-halûḥît bĕḇekî yaʿăleh-bô): the ascent of Luhith (also cited in Isaiah 15:5) and the road down to Horonaim are the two main routes of Moab — one going up, one going down — and on both the refugees flee in continuous weeping (bĕḇekî tamrûrîm, with bitter weeping) and cries of anguish (ṣārê-yĕlalelû šĕḇer). The geography of Moab's road system becomes the geography of its mourning.
6: Nûsû mallĕṭû napšĕḵem ûḵĕʿarôʿēr bammidbar tihyû (ûḵĕʿarôʿēr bammidbar tihyû): "Flee! Save yourselves! Be like a desert shrub (ʿarôʿēr) in the wilderness." The word ʿarôʿēr (juniper/tamarisk, a solitary shrub) is the same used in 17:6 for the man who trusts in human beings instead of YHWH: "he is like a shrub in the desert and shall not see any good come." By calling Moab to become what the cursed man is, the oracle identifies Moab's condition as the direct consequence of misplaced trust (v7: "because you trusted in your works").
7: Kî yaʿan biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ gam-ʾat tillaḵēdî (Kî yaʿan biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ): the theological root of Moab's judgment — trust (bāṭaḥ) in her own works (maʿăśāyim, both deeds and manufactured goods/idols) and in her storehouses (ʾôṣĕrôt, treasure-houses, reserves). This is the antithesis of the trust YHWH requires (cf. Jeremiah 17:7-8: blessed is the person who trusts in YHWH). Wĕyāṣāʾ Kĕmôš baggōlâ hûʾ wĕkōhănāyw wĕśārāyw yaḥdāw (Wĕyāṣāʾ Kĕmôš baggōlâ): Chemosh, Moab's national deity (Numbers 21:29; Judges 11:24; 1 Kings 11:7, 33) goes into exile along with his priests and officials. The defeat of a people's patron deity is the ultimate OAN statement: Chemosh cannot protect Moab because he himself is carried off as conquered territory. Cf. Isaiah 46:1-2 (Bel and Nebo borne away on beasts of burden).
8: Wĕyāḇôʾ šōdēd ʾel-kol-ʿîr wĕʿîr lōʾ timmāleṭ wĕʾāḇad haʿēmeq wĕnišmad hammîšôr kaʾăšer dibber YHWH (kaʾăšer dibber YHWH): "the destroyer will come to every city; not one city will escape... exactly as YHWH has decreed." The oracle's completion formula (kaʾăšer dibber YHWH) anchors the prediction's authority: what has happened/will happen fulfills the divine word. The completeness — every city (kol-ʿîr wĕʿîr), not one escaping (lōʾ timmāleṭ) — gives the valley and plateau no shelter.
9: Tĕnû ṣîṣ lĕMôʾāḇ kî nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ (Tĕnû ṣîṣ lĕMôʾāḇ kî nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ): the command "give wings to Moab so she may fly away" is sardonic — Moab will need wings because flight is the only remaining option. nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ (the doubled infinitive absolute: she will surely fly away) uses the doubled form to express certainty; the irony is that the only "flight" available is the hopeless flight of refugee flight, not the soaring of freedom. Wĕʿārêhā lĕšammâ tihyênâ mēʾên yôšēḇ bāhen (lĕšammâ tihyênâ mēʾên yôšēḇ): the desolation formula — cities becoming šammâ (desolation) without inhabitants — is the same formula applied to Jerusalem (Jeremiah 4:7) and Egypt (Jeremiah 46:19). No people is exempt from this consequence of covenant/divine-law violation.
10: ʾĀrûr ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ wĕʾārûr mōnēaʿ ḥarbô middām (ʾĀrûr ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ wĕʾārûr mōnēaʿ ḥarbô middām): a striking verse — "cursed is the one who carries out YHWH's work negligently, and cursed is the one who holds his sword back from bloodshed." The oracle uses the covenant-curse formula (ʾārûr, Deut 27-28) to bind the Babylonian executors of judgment to full implementation. rĕmiyyâ (slackness, negligence, deceit) — the ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ (one who does YHWH's work with slackness) is cursed along with the one who withholds violence. This verse creates significant theological tension — YHWH binding human soldiers to thoroughness in execution — but it reflects the consistent OAN theology that the conquering army is YHWH's instrument, not an autonomous agent.
11: Šaqaṭ Môʾāḇ minĕʿûrāyw wĕšōqēṭ hûʾ ʿal-šĕmārāyw wĕlōʾ-hûraq mikkĕlî ʾel-kēlî ûḇaggōlâ lōʾ-hālāḵ (wĕlōʾ-hûraq mikkĕlî ʾel-kēlî): the wine-on-its-dregs metaphor is the most distinctive image in the Moab oracle. Moab is like undisturbed wine that has never been poured from vessel to vessel — unfiltered wine left on its sediment develops a corrupted, stale flavor. The refinement process of pouring wine from jar to jar (to separate it from the lees/dregs, šĕmārîm) is what creates clarity and quality. ʿAl-kēn ʿāmad ṭaʿmô bô wĕrêḥô lōʾ nāmār (ʿāmad ṭaʿmô bô wĕrêḥô lōʾ nāmār): "his flavor has stayed in him and his aroma has not changed" — the metaphor: Moab's undisturbed prosperity (no exile, no external pressure) has produced cultural/moral stagnation. Peace without discipline produces corruption rather than character. Cf. Psalms 55:19 ("those who have no changes dread not God").
12: Lāḵēn hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕšillaḥtî-lô ṣōʿîm wĕṣĕrûhû ûkĕlêhû yāreqqû wĕnibĕlêhem yĕnappēṣû (wĕšillaḥtî-lô ṣōʿîm wĕṣĕrûhû): "I will send wine-tilters (ṣōʿîm, those who tilt/pour) who will tip him over and empty his vessels and smash his jars." The YHWH-as-vintner sends agents to do what should have been done through natural historical process — the exile (being poured from vessel to vessel) that Moab escaped becomes a violent compulsory process. wĕnibĕlêhem yĕnappēṣû (and his containers — nēḇel, wine-jug — will be smashed): the smashed wine-jug image echoes Jeremiah's own sign-act with the pottery flask (baqbûq) in Jeremiah 19 — the broken jar as irreversible judgment.
13: Wĕḇôš Môʾāḇ miKKĕmôš kaʾăšer-bôšû bêt-yiśrāʾēl mibBêt ʾēl miḇṭaḥām (kaʾăšer-bôšû bêt-yiśrāʾēl mibBêt ʾēl miḇṭaḥām): the comparison to Israel's shame over Bethel is historically precise — the golden calves established by Jeroboam I at Bethel and Dan (1 Kings 12:28-29) were the defining apostasy of the northern kingdom. Israel trusted (bāṭaḥ) in Bethel's bulls; they were proved worthless. Moab trusted in Chemosh; Chemosh too will be proved worthless. The comparison democratizes the experience of idolatrous shame: Israel's history of false trust is the template for the nations' judgment.
14: Êḵ tōmĕrû ʾănaḥnû gibbōrîm wĕʾanšê-ḥayil lammilḥāmâ (ʾănaḥnû gibbōrîm wĕʾanšê-ḥayil lammilḥāmâ): the rhetorical question challenges Moab's self-description as "heroes, battle-hardened men" (gibbōrîm... ʾanšê-ḥayil). The vocabulary of military prowess — gibbôr (mighty warrior), ʾîš ḥayil (man of valor) — is the language of Moab's own boasting. This sets up the inversion: the claim of might makes the collapse more devastating. Êḵ (How?) — the same funeral-lament interrogative used in the book of Lamentations (Jeremiah 1:1; Jeremiah 2:1; Jeremiah 4:1) — frames the self-congratulatory claim as already past, a subject of lamentation.
15: Šuddad Môʾāḇ wĕʿārêhā ʿālâ ûmibḥar baḥûrāyw yārĕdû laṭṭāḇaḥ (wĕʿārêhā ʿālâ ûmibḥar baḥûrāyw yārĕdû laṭṭāḇaḥ): the devastation is comprehensive — the cities are overrun and the choicest (mibḥar) young men go down to slaughter (laṭṭāḇaḥ, the slaughterhouse term; cf. Isaiah 53:7; Jeremiah 11:19 — both uses of the slaughter-lamb). Nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô (Nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô): "declares the King, the LORD of hosts is his name" — the divine warrior title (YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt) with hammelek (the King) combines to present YHWH as the sovereign who commands both the cosmic army and the judgment of nations. Cf. Malachi 1:14 ("I am a great King, says the LORD of hosts").
16: Qārôḇ ʾêd Môʾāḇ lāḇôʾ wĕrāʿātô māhĕrâ mĕʾōd (Qārôḇ ʾêd Môʾāḇ lāḇôʾ wĕrāʿātô māhĕrâ mĕʾōd): the catastrophe is described as imminent — "Moab's disaster is close at hand and his ruin coming very quickly." ʾêd (disaster, doom, calamity) appears in the Wisdom literature (Proverbs 1:26-27; Jeremiah 6:15; Jeremiah 24:22) for sudden catastrophe; in Jeremiah 18:17 YHWH shows the back not the face on the day of their ʾêd. The speed (māhĕrâ mĕʾōd) and proximity (qārôḇ) together convey the urgency of irreversibility — there is no more time for repentance.
17: Nûdû lô kol-sĕḇîḇāyw wĕḵol-yōdĕʿê šĕmô ʾimrû ʾêḵ nišbar maṭṭēh-ʿōz maqqēl tipʾāret (ʾêḵ nišbar maṭṭēh-ʿōz maqqēl tipʾāret): the funeral lament formula — ʾêḵ (how!) + passive verb of destruction. The "powerful scepter" (maṭṭēh-ʿōz) and "glorious staff" (maqqēl tipʾāret) are regalia of royal power — Moab's imperial sovereignty is the scepter/staff that is broken. Cf. Ezekiel 19:10-14 (the lament over Judah's scepter/vine); Isaiah 9:4; Jeremiah 10:24 (breaking the rod of oppression). The lament is addressed to those who surround Moab and know its name — a call to communal mourning for what was, rather than what Moab became.
18: Rĕdî miqqāḇôd wĕšĕḇî baṣṣāmāʾ yōšeḇet bat-Dîḇôn (Rĕdî miqqāḇôd wĕšĕḇî baṣṣāmāʾ): "Come down from your glory and sit in thirst, O inhabitants of Dibon." Kāḇôd (glory, weight, honor) down to ṣāmāʾ (thirst, drought) — the polarity is absolute: from the height of national glory to the parched ground of refugee thirst. Dibon was Mesha's royal city (the Mesha Stele describes his building activities there, ~850 BCE). Kî šōdēd Môʾāḇ ʿālâ ḇāḵ šiḥat miḇṣārāyiḵ (kî šōdēd Môʾāḇ ʿālâ ḇāḵ šiḥat miḇṣārāyiḵ): the destroyer has come up against Dibon's fortresses (miḇṣārîm, fortified places) and ruined them.
19: ʿimĕdî hadd-dereḵ ûṣappî yōšeḇet ʿarôʿēr šaʾălî nās ûmāleṭ ʾimrî mah-nîhyâ (ʿimĕdî hadd-dereḵ ûṣappî... šaʾălî nās ûmāleṭ): the command to Aroer (on the Arnon River, the southern boundary of Moab's territory, Deuteronomy 2:36; Jeremiah 3:12) to stand by the road and question the fleeing and escaping: "What has happened?" (mah-nîhyâ). The question is rhetorical — the questioner will receive a report of total collapse. The posting of a town at the crossroads to gather news from refugees mirrors the way ancient Near Eastern cities functioned as information nodes during military campaigns.
20: Hōḇîš Môʾāḇ kî ḥattâ hêlîlî ûzʿāqî haggîdû ʾaḇ-ʾARNÔN kî šuddad Môʾāḇ (hêlîlî ûzʿāqî haggîdû ʾaḇ-ʾARNÔN kî šuddad Môʾāḇ): the lamentation intensifies — Moab is shamed, shattered; the command is to wail, cry, and announce it at the Arnon. hêlîl (wail, howl — the funeral cry used in Isaiah 13:6; Ezekiel 21:12; Amos 8:3; Joel 1:5) is the distinctive sound of prophetic judgment literature, the vocalization of šammâ (desolation). The announcement at the Arnon (Moab's northern boundary with Israel) signals the collapse is complete from border to border.
21: Ûmišpāṭ bāʾ ʾel-ʾereṣ hammîšôr ʾel-Ḥôlôn wĕʾel-Yahṣâ wĕʿal-Môpāʿat (Ûmišpāṭ bāʾ ʾel-ʾereṣ hammîšôr): the long list of Moabite cities (vv21-24) to which judgment comes exemplifies the prophetic list-curse. Each city-name carries the rhetorical weight of a funeral toll. Hammîšôr (the Plateau/Tableland) is the high Transjordanian plateau east of the Dead Sea — the heartland of Moab's agriculture and settlement. Holon, Jahzah (site of Israel's battle with Sihon, Numbers 21:23), and Mephaath are named in the Reubenite city lists (Joshua 13:17-21).
22: Wĕʿal-Dîḇôn wĕʿal-Nĕḇô wĕʿal-Bêt-Diḇlātāyim: Dibon (Mesha's royal city), Nebo (where the inscription found in 1868 naming Moab was excavated near Mount Nebo), and Beth-diblathaim continue the city-by-city roll call of judgment. Each name is a place where real people lived, farmed, and prayed — the oracle's specificity prevents it from being abstracted into generic judgment rhetoric. This is the same technique Jeremiah uses for Jerusalem's own doom (Jeremiah 30:4-7; Jeremiah 51:54).
23: Wĕʿal-Qiryātāyim wĕʿal-Bêt-Gāmûl wĕʿal-Bêt-Mĕʿôn: Kiriathaim (mentioned in v1), Beth-gamul (unidentified but Moabite), and Beth-meon (also called Baal-meon, Numbers 32:38; Ezekiel 25:9 — a city whose very name embeds the Baal cult). The Baal-meon connection anticipates v35's oracle against Moabite high-place religion.
24: Wĕʿal-Qĕriyyôt wĕʿal-Boṣrâ wĕʿal kol-ʿārê ʾereṣ-Môʾāḇ hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt (hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt): the list concludes with Kerioth and Bozrah (the latter appears also in the Edom oracle, Jeremiah 49:13, 22; Amos 1:12) and the comprehensive formula "all the cities of the land of Moab, far and near." hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt (the distant and the near) — geographic totality, the formula of complete coverage (cf. Deuteronomy 13:7; Jeremiah 29:22; 1 Kings 8:46).
25: Nîgdĕʿâ qeren Môʾāḇ ûzĕrōʿô nišbārâ nĕʾum-YHWH (Nîgdĕʿâ qeren Môʾāḇ ûzĕrōʿô nišbārâ): the oracle-within-a-list: qeren (horn, symbol of power and pride — Psalms 75:5-10; Lamentations 2:3; Amos 6:13) and zĕrôaʿ (arm, symbol of military strength) are hewn off and broken. The two images together cover both the external display of power (horn as crown-symbol) and the instrument of power (arm). YHWH as the one who breaks the nations' power is the theological thread running through the entire OAN collection.
26: Hašḵîrûhû kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô wĕhāyâ lĭśĕḥōq gam-hûʾ (kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl): the sin specified — Moab "exalted himself against YHWH" (higdîl ʿal-YHWH). The Hiphil of gādal (to make oneself great/lifted above) applied to ʿal-YHWH (against/above YHWH) is the defining sin of hubris — positioning oneself above or in opposition to YHWH's sovereignty. Wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô wĕhāyâ lĭśĕḥōq gam-hûʾ (wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô): "Moab will wallow in his own vomit" — the drunkenness image (hašḵîrûhû, make him drunk) leads to the degradation of vomiting in his own filth, becoming an object of ridicule (śĕḥōq). Cf. Habakkuk 2:16: shame covers glory when the cup of YHWH's wrath is drunk.
27: Wĕʾim-lōʾ lĭśĕḥōq hāyâ lĕḵā yiśrāʾēl ʾim-biggannāḇîm nimṣāʾ (Wĕʾim-lōʾ lĭśĕḥōq hāyâ lĕḵā yiśrāʾēl ʾim-biggannāḇîm nimṣāʾ): the oracle turns the mockery back on Moab — "Was not Israel a laughingstock to you? Was he found among thieves?" The question carries the logic of judgment's reversal: Moab mocked Israel in her humiliation; now Moab will be mocked. Kî-midday dibbārĕḵā bô tinnôdēd (kî-midday dibbārĕḵā bô tinnôdēd): "For whenever you spoke of him you wagged your head (tinnôdēd, the head-shaking gesture of contempt, Psalms 22:7; Lamentations 2:15)" — Moab's contemptuous commentary on Israel's fall is what makes Moab's own fall a matter of divine justice-in-kind.
28: ʿIzĕḇû ʿārîm wĕšiḵnû bassela yōšĕḇê Môʾāḇ wĕhiyyû kĕyônâ tĕqannēn bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat (wĕhiyyû kĕyônâ tĕqannēn bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat): "Be like the dove that builds its nest deep in the walls of a ravine." The dove (yônâ) nesting deep in the cliff-walls (bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat, on the far side of the mouth of the pit/ravine) is an image of the most hidden, inaccessible nesting — Moab must seek the deepest hiding possible, because the cities offer no protection. Cf. Song of Solomon 2:14 ("my dove in the clefts of the rock") — the same image of cliff-refuge, there used lovingly; here it is counsel of desperate concealment.
29: Šāmaʿnû gĕʾôn-Môʾāḇ gēʾeh mĕʾōd gabbĕhûtô wĕgaʾăwātô wĕgĕʾônô wĕrûm libbô (Šāmaʿnû gĕʾôn-Môʾāḇ gēʾeh mĕʾōd): five words for Moab's pride in a single verse — gĕʾôn (majesty/pride), gēʾeh (haughty), gabbĕhût (loftiness), gaʾăwâ (arrogance), rûm lēḇ (exaltation of heart). The concentration creates a portrait of a nation whose self-assessment was its central feature — and its fatal flaw. Cf. Isaiah 16:6 (the parallel passage): "We have heard of the pride of Moab — how proud he is! — of his arrogance, his pride, and his insolence." Moab's pride is the standing OT example of national hubris against YHWH (cf. also Ezekiel 25:8-11).
30: ʾĂnî yādaʿtî nĕʾum-YHWH ʿebrātô wĕlōʾ-ḵēn baddāyw lōʾ-ḵēn ʿāśû (ʾĂnî yādaʿtî nĕʾum-YHWH ʿebrātô): "I know his arrogance, declares YHWH — but it comes to nothing; his empty boasts accomplish nothing." YHWH's declaration of knowledge (yādaʿtî, I know) is the divine omniscient assessment. ʿebrâ (arrogance, overflow — Proverbs 11:4; Jeremiah 21:24) is what YHWH knows; the double lōʾ-ḵēn ("not so, not so") is a dismissal: Moab's bluster is assessed, found hollow, and dismissed. YHWH's calm knowledge of Moab's pride contrasts with the dramatic military collapse described elsewhere in the chapter.
31: Lāḵēn ʿal-Môʾāḇ ʾeylelāh wĕlĕḵol-Môʾāḇ ʾizʿāq lĕʾanšê Qîr-ḥereś ʾehgeh (Lāḵēn ʿal-Môʾāḇ ʾeylelāh): the divine lament — YHWH himself wails (ʾeylelāh, Qal imperfect of yālal, to wail) over Moab. This is theologically remarkable: the God who pronounces judgment over Moab also mourns over it. Cf. Jeremiah 48:31 paralleling Isaiah 16:7 and the divine weeping in Isaiah 16:9-11. The same tension appears in Lam 3 (YHWH is both the agent of destruction and the one who does not abandon). The divine lament is not contradiction but the full expression of YHWH's character: judgment and compassion coexist.
32: ʾEt-bĕḵî yĕʿaZĒR ʾeḇkeh-lāḵ gepen Śiḇmâ nĕṭîšōtayiḵ ʿāḇrû yam ʿad-yam yĕʿaZĒR higgîʿû (gepen Śiḇmâ nĕṭîšōtayiḵ ʿāḇrû yam): the lament over Sibmah's vines — the famous vineyards that spread their tendrils across the sea (the Dead Sea) to Jazer — is a regional elegy for agricultural abundance destroyed. ʿal-qayiṣēḵ wĕʿal-bĕṣîrĕḵā šōdēd nāpāl (šōdēd nāpāl): the destroyer has fallen on the summer fruit and vintage — harvest imagery for the destruction of Moab's agricultural identity. Cf. Isaiah 16:8-10 (the lament over Sibmah's vines in the parallel OAN).
33: Wĕneʾespâ śimḥâ wāgîl mikkarmēl ûmēʾereṣ Môʾāḇ ûyayim miyyĕqāḇîm hibbattî yôdēd lōʾ-yôdēd lōʾ hēdād yēdād (yayim miyyĕqāḇîm hibbattî yôdēd lōʾ-yôdēd): joy and gladness stripped from the fertile fields. Yôdēd (the harvest shout, the treaders' cry) — "the only shout is not a harvest shout." The harvest treading song (hēdād, shouting) is silenced; what replaces it is the silence of lōʾ hēdād (no shout). The agricultural calendar of joy — harvest festivals, communal celebration — is extinguished. Cf. Joel 1:11-12 (similar lament over agricultural devastation). Joy and gladness (śimḥâ wāgîl) are YHWH's gifts that can be withdrawn.
34: Mizzĕʿaqat Ḥešbôn ʿad-ʾĔlāʿēlēh ʿad-Yahaz nātĕnû qôlām miṣṣōʿar ʿad-Ḥōrônāyim ʿeglat šĕlîšiyyâ kî gam-mê Nimrîm lĭmšammôt yihyû (kî gam-mê Nimrîm lĭmšammôt yihyû): the sweep of the cry from Heshbon to Elealeh (NE of Heshbon), to Jahaz, Zoar (near the S end of the Dead Sea), to Horonaim — this geographic traversal names the major northsouth axis of Moab. ʿeglat šĕlîšiyyâ ("like a three-year-old heifer's bellow") — the bellowing of a fully grown, unsacrificed heifer is the image for the loudness of the cry. Even the waters of Nimrim (probably the Wadi Nimrin, north of the Dead Sea) will be dried up — the fundamental resources of water and agriculture fail together.
35: Wĕhišbattî lĕMôʾāḇ nĕʾum-YHWH maʿăleh bāmâ wĕmaqṭîr lēʾlōhāyw (Wĕhišbattî lĕMôʾāḇ... maʿăleh bāmâ wĕmaqṭîr lēʾlōhāyw): YHWH will put an end in Moab to anyone who brings offerings to the high places (bāmôt) and burns incense to his gods (lēʾlōhāyw). The Moabite high places and their cult are the specific target of divine abolition — the same judgment language used for Israel's syncretistic worship (1 Kings 11:7; 2 Kings 23:13-14). Chemosh was worshiped at high places throughout Moab; their elimination marks the end of Moab's national-religious identity.
36: Lāḵēn libbî lĕMôʾāḇ kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh ûlibbî ʾel-ʾanšê Qîr-ḥereś kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh (libbî lĕMôʾāḇ kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh): "my heart wails for Moab like flutes" — the divine heart (lēḇ) wailing like funeral flutes (ḥălîlîm, pipe-flutes used at funerals; Matthew 9:23 refers to flute-players at death) is the OT expression of divine compassion within judgment. The double repetition ("my heart wails for Moab like flutes... my heart wails like flutes for the men of Kir-hareseth") creates a liturgical lament. ʿal-kēn yitrat ʿāśâ ʾāḇādû (yitrat ʿāśâ ʾāḇādû): "for the riches they gathered have perished" — the accumulated wealth that Moab trusted (v7: biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ) is gone.
37: Kî ḵol-rōʾš qorḥâ wĕḵol-zāqān gĕrûʿâ ʿal-kol-yādāyim gĕdûdōt ûʿal-mŏtnāyim śāq (kî ḵol-rōʾš qorḥâ wĕḵol-zāqān gĕrûʿâ): the comprehensive mourning rites — every head shaved (qorḥâ), every beard cut (gĕrûʿâ), gashes on every hand (gĕdûdōt), sackcloth around every waist — enumerate all four of the standard ancient Near Eastern mourning gestures (cf. Isaiah 15:2-3; Ezekiel 27:30-31; Amos 8:10). The universal coverage ("every" repeated four times) signals that Moab's entire population is in mourning — no exceptions, no exemptions.
38: ʿAl-kol-gaggôt Môʾāḇ ûbirḥōḇōtêhā kulloh misped kî šāḇartî ʾet-Môʾāḇ kikĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ bô nĕʾum-YHWH (šāḇartî ʾet-Môʾāḇ kikĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ bô): YHWH's first-person claim: "I have shattered Moab like a pot no one wants" — kĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ (vessel in which there is no pleasure/desire) is a broken, worthless piece of pottery. The same image of the worthless broken vessel appears in Hosea 8:8 ("Israel is swallowed up; among the nations he is like a vessel no one wants") and Psalms 31:12. The pottery imagery throughout Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:10; 18-19; 32; 45) reaches its final OAN expression here: Moab, like the nations, like Jerusalem, is clay in the potter YHWH's hands — broken when he chooses.
39: Êḵ ḥattâ hêlîlû êḵ-hipnâ ʿōrep Môʾāḇ bôš wĕhāyâ Môʾāḇ lĭśĕḥōq ûlĕmaḥittâ lĕḵol-sĕḇîḇāyw (êḵ-hipnâ ʿōrep Môʾāḇ bôš): the lament (êḵ ḥattâ hêlîlû, "how it is shattered — wail!") and the specific humiliation — Moab turned its back (ʿōrep) in shame. The same vocabulary used for Israel's apostasy (Jeremiah 2:27; Jeremiah 32:33: "they turned their back to me, not their face") applies to Moab's military flight. The turning of the back in battle is both physical retreat and symbolic rejection — shame embodied in posture. lĭśĕḥōq ûlĕmaḥittâ (a laughingstock and a terror) — the reversal of Moab's own contemptuous mockery of Israel (v27).
40: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh kaṇṇešer yidʾeh ûpāraś kĕnāpāyw ʾel-Môʾāḇ (hinnēh kaṇṇešer yidʾeh ûpāraś kĕnāpāyw ʾel-Môʾāḇ): the eagle simile for the conqueror — YHWH (or Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH's agent) swoops like a nešer (eagle/vulture, the largest and most powerful raptor of the ANE) spreading wings over Moab. The same image appears for Babylon's invasion of Judah (Ezekiel 17:3: "a great eagle came to Lebanon"; Habakkuk 1:8: "their horses fly like eagles") and for Edom (Jeremiah 49:22; Lamentations 4:19). The eagle/vulture embodies the irreversible, airborne speed of military conquest.
41: Nilkĕdâ haqqĕriyyôt wĕhammĕṣādôt niltāḥû wĕhāyâ lēḇ gibborê Môʾāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ kĕlēḇ ʾiššâ mĕṣērâ (wĕhāyâ lēḇ gibborê Môʾāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ kĕlēḇ ʾiššâ mĕṣērâ): Kerioth and the fortresses taken; the hearts of Moab's warriors are like the heart of a woman in labor (mĕṣērâ, the one in labor-pain). The labor-pain simile for terror and helplessness recurs throughout the OAN (Jeremiah 46:24; Jeremiah 49:22-24; Jeremiah 50:43) and Israel's own judgment (Jeremiah 4:31; Jeremiah 13:21; Jeremiah 22:23; Jeremiah 30:6) — it crosses gender boundaries to describe absolute vulnerability and loss of control.
42: Wĕniśmad Môʾāḇ mēʿam kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl (kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl): the sin summarized — "Moab will be destroyed as a nation because he exalted himself against YHWH." The causal kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl (because he magnified himself against YHWH) is the oracle's theological thesis. This is the same sin identified in Babylon's judgment (Jeremiah 50:24, 29, 31-32), in the king of Babylon's taunt (Isaiah 14:13-14), and in Nebuchadnezzar's narrative (Daniel 4:30). National hubris against YHWH's sovereignty is the universal grounds for judgment in the prophetic literature.
43: Paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ ʿālêḵā yôšēḇ Môʾāḇ nĕʾum-YHWH (Paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ ʿālêḵā yôšēḇ Môʾāḇ): the alliterative triple of terror — paḥad (terror, dread), paḥat (pit), and pāḥ (trap/snare) — three p/ph sounds creating the acoustic sensation of inescapable disaster. Isaiah 24:17 uses the identical formula (paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ) for the universal judgment of the earth in the apocalyptic oracle. Jeremiah's use here intensifies the scope: what Isaiah applied to the whole earth, Jeremiah applies to Moab as a national representative of the principle.
44: Hannās mippĕnê happāḥad yippōl ʾel-happāḥat wĕhaʿōleh min-happāḥat yillāḵēd bappāḥ kî ʾāḇîʾ ʾēlêhā ʾel-Môʾāḇ šĕnat pĕquddātām nĕʾum-YHWH (Hannās mippĕnê happāḥad yippōl ʾel-happāḥat): the three-word sequence from v43 becomes a three-stage scenario — the one who flees terror (pāḥad) falls into the pit (paḥat); the one who climbs out of the pit is caught in the trap (pāḥ). No escape route exists: flight leads to the pit, climbing out leads to the snare. This is the logic of inescapable divine judgment (cf. Amos 5:19: "as if a man fled from a lion and a bear met him, or went into the house and a serpent bit him"). šĕnat pĕquddātām (the year of their reckoning) — YHWH's appointed time of visitation, the same phrase used for Egypt's judgment (Jeremiah 46:21).
45: Bĕṣēl Ḥešbôn ʿāmĕdû mēḵōaḥ nāsîm kî-ʾēš yāṣāʾ mēḤešbôn wĕlehāḇâ mibbên Sîḥôn wattōʾḵal peʾat Môʾāḇ ûqodqod bĕnê-šāʾôn (kî-ʾēš yāṣāʾ mēḤešbôn wĕlehāḇâ mibbên Sîḥôn): an ancient poem about the Amorite king Sihon from Numbers 21:27-29 is cited — fire from Heshbon and flame from Sihon's midst devouring Moab. The citation of the old war-poem integrates the ancient tradition of Moab's vulnerability: the prophetic judgment is not new but the culmination of a long history of conflict. Sihon (bên Sîḥôn, within/from Sihon) was the Amorite king who defeated Moab before being himself defeated by Israel (Numbers 21:21-30). History's wheel turns through YHWH's sovereign direction.
46: Ôy-lĕḵā Môʾāḇ ʾāḇad ʿam Kĕmôš kî luqqĕḥû bānêḵā baššĕḇî ûḇĕnōtêḵā baššiḇyâ (ʾāḇad ʿam Kĕmôš): "the people of Chemosh are destroyed" — the identification of Moab's national identity with Chemosh (ʿam Kĕmôš, people of Chemosh) echoes Numbers 21:29: "Woe to you, Moab! You are ruined, O people of Chemosh." The oracle cites the ancient lament verbatim, completing the literary inclusio: what ancient poets saw in Moab's defeat by Sihon, Jeremiah now announces as YHWH's final judgment. kî luqqĕḥû bānêḵā baššĕḇî ûḇĕnōtêḵā baššiḇyâ: sons captured, daughters led captive — the social destruction of the family, the dismantling of future generations.
47: Wĕšaḇtî šĕḇût-Môʾāḇ bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm nĕʾum-YHWH ʿad-hēnnâ mišpaṭ Môʾāḇ (Wĕšaḇtî šĕḇût-Môʾāḇ bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm): the chapter ends with an eschatological restoration footnote — "Yet in the latter days I will restore the fortunes of Moab, declares YHWH." šāḇ šĕḇût (restore the fortunes) is the restoration formula used for Israel (Jeremiah 30:3; Jeremiah 31:23; Jeremiah 32:44; Jeremiah 33:7, 11, 26) and here extended to Moab, Ammon (Jeremiah 49:6), and Egypt (Jeremiah 46:26). The inclusion of Moab's future restoration within the judgment oracle is consistent with YHWH's universal redemptive horizon — judgment is not the last word even for Moab. Paul's universalism in Romans 11:32 ("God has consigned all to disobedience so that he may have mercy on all") resonates with this trajectory: the same divine sovereignty that judges is the sovereignty that restores. ʿad-hēnnâ mišpaṭ Môʾāḇ: "This is the end of the judgment of Moab" — a formal closure formula marking the oracle's completion.
